title: Impairment
description: Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS vs IFRS vs US GAAP

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Impairment recognized if carrying amount > undiscounted future cash flows; loss = difference to fair value.
Single-step model: impair if carrying amount > recoverable amount (higher of fair value less costs to sell and value in use).
Two-step for long-lived assets: (1) Recoverability test (undiscounted CFs), (2) Loss = fair value - carrying amount. Goodwill: qualitative or quantitative test at reporting unit level.
From undiscounted CF triggers to discounted recoverable amount / fair value.
Significant change in operations/market, decline in asset value, or physical damage.
Indications at each reporting date (external + internal).
Goodwill tested annually or more often if trigger; long-lived assets tested only when triggers exist.
IFRS/Ind AS more proactive; US GAAP annual goodwill test required.
Individual asset or cash generating unit (CGU) loosely defined.
CGU = smallest group generating independent cash inflows.
Long-lived assets: asset group level; Goodwill: reporting unit (one level below segment).
IFRS CGU is more operational; US GAAP reporting unit is more financial-segment-aligned.
Not explicitly defined; loss = carrying amount - fair value (if estimable).
Higher of fair value less costs of disposal (FVLCD) and value in use (VIU = PV of future CFs).
Fair value (market price or DCF) used in step 2; no value in use concept.
Ind AS/IFRS use VIU; US GAAP fair value only.
Used only in step 2 fair value measurement.
Discounting embedded in core impairment math under new standards.
Prohibited for goodwill; allowed for other assets if recoverable amount increases.
Prohibited for all assets (once impaired, carrying amount cannot be increased).
Key divergence: US GAAP most conservative; AS most flexible.
Amortized + tested for impairment if trigger.
Not amortized; tested annually; one-step: compare CGU carrying amount (incl. goodwill) vs recoverable amount.
Not amortized; tested annually; two-step (or qualitative option): compare reporting unit fair value vs carrying amount.
Goodwill treated as a permanent asset under new standards, but tested rigorously.
Allocate to CGUs expected to benefit from synergy.
Allocate to reporting units at acquisition.
Systematic allocation required under all new standards.
Same as tangible; no special rules.
Same rules as tangibles; indefinite life intangibles tested annually.
Indefinite life intangibles tested annually like goodwill; finite life tested on triggers.
Indefinite-lived intangibles get separate attention under new rules.
Extensive: CGU details, discount rates, sensitivity analysis, assumptions.
Extensive but less required sensitivity than IFRS; fair value hierarchy disclosure.
IFRS/Ind AS more narrative-heavy; US GAAP more quantitative fair value focus.

Triggering Test (Step 1) Carrying amount (50 Cr) > Undiscounted CFs (48 Cr) - Impairment required Measurement (Step 2) Impairment loss = Carrying amount - Fair value = 20 Cr Dr Impairment loss 20 CrCr Accumulated impairment 20 Cr Post-Impairment Carrying Amount 30 Cr Future Depreciation 6.00 Cr/year

Trigger & Recoverable Amount Recoverable amount = Higher of FVLCD (30 Cr) and VIU (34 Cr) = 34 Cr Measurement Impairment loss = Carrying amount (50 Cr) - Recoverable amount (34 Cr) = 16 Cr Dr Impairment loss 16 CrCr Accumulated impairment 16 Cr Post-Impairment Carrying Amount 34 Cr Future Depreciation 6.80 Cr/year

In Accordance with IAS 36 Fully aligned with Ind AS 36. Single-step impairment comparison with recoverable amount (higher of value-in-use and fair value less costs to sell): No preliminary recoverability test step matches US GAAP style Reversals allowed if recovery estimates change Same outcomes as Ind AS 36: Impairment loss of 16 Cr, with future depreciation adjusted to 6.80 Cr/year.

Triggering Test (Step 1) Carrying amount (50 Cr) > Undiscounted CFs (48 Cr) - Fail - Step 2 required Measurement (Step 2) Impairment loss = Carrying amount - Fair value = 50 Cr - 32 Cr = 18 Cr Dr Impairment loss 18 CrCr Accumulated impairment 18 Cr Post-Impairment Carrying Amount 32 Cr
No for goodwill; yes for others

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - Year 1

tableData: Net operating cash unimpacted by non-cash impairment
Total cash drain is identical across all frameworks
Return on assets mechanically shifts as carrying amounts change
Reported equity base drops, depressing returns
Velocity of assets appears faster due to written-down denominator
Leverage artificially worsens as retained earnings take the hit

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: Impairment loss lower under Ind AS/IFRS than AS
Discounting in VIU reduces loss compared to undiscounted CF test
New standard may show smaller hits but still real economics
Recompute impairment using standardized discount rate across peers
US GAAP impairment higher than IFRS
Fair value only, no VIU benefit
US GAAP more conservative for tangible assets
IFRS/Ind AS prohibit reversal for goodwill; US GAAP prohibits all reversals
Do not expect future write-ups; track acquisition discipline
Compare timing of triggers to peer and industry cycle
Request discount rate and growth assumption details
Exclude non-recurring impairment for core earnings
Use pre-impairment asset base for trend
Track adjusted book value excluding impairment
Compare CFO before and after impairment
Recalculate using impaired asset base for future
Impairment signals past overpayment or obsolescence
Leverage looks worse but cash flow unaffected
Why is impairment lower under Ind AS/IFRS than AS?
Discounting in VIU reduces present value of future CFs, so recoverable amount higher
VIU uses 12% discount rate vs undiscounted AS test
Yes, triggers are judgmental; but auditors challenge large misses
Compare impairment timing to industry downturns
Why no reversal under US GAAP?
US GAAP prohibits reversal for all assets; IFRS allows reversal for tangibles
Check if asset value truly recovers
How to compare goodwill impairment across IFRS and US GAAP?
IFRS one-step (CGU vs recoverable); US GAAP two-step (fair value vs carrying amount of reporting unit)
Recompute goodwill using unified fair value approach
Use WACC or peer discount rates for sanity check
Recognize impairment is often non-cash and past-looking
Rebuild ROIC on post-impairment asset base
Use impairment as signal of management honesty
Monitor covenants that include book value
Underwrite future cash flows ignoring sunk cost
Takes large goodwill impairment right after acquisition
Asset value recovered; but management may be optimistic
Uses high discount rate for VIU
Test with your own CF model

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Repeated 'one-time' impairment charges over multiple years
Sign of structural capital misallocation. Apply a permanent 'management discount' to valuation.
Impairment occurs immediately after a change in CEO/CFO or acquisition
Classic 'kitchen sinking' to reset the baseline for future bonuses.
Discount rate used in impairment testing is suspiciously low
Management is artificially shielding assets from impairment. Recalculate with market rates.
Detailed sensitivity analysis provided for carrying amounts
Allows you to independently stress-test the balance sheet carrying values.
Impairment taken proactively ahead of industry cycle deterioration
Sign of honest, conservative management facing reality early.
